title: Public Works Committee
overview: The Public Works Committee approved several key items including a resolution supporting state transportation funding, awarded a recycled asphalt crushing contract to King Gravel for $126,900, and directed staff to create enforcement language for sanitary sewer discharge violations with fines of $100/day. The committee also approved construction agreements for multiple road projects and received updates on infrastructure grants totaling over $9 million in recent years.

agenda.item: Acknowledge April 2026 water and sewer permits
2025 Water Utility PSC annual report review
Water and sewer connection agreement - Radkkey Avenue

discussions.body: Michael reported the village received a Local Road Improvement Grant for Birch Street reconstruction between Scoffield Avenue and Community Center Drive, covering over 50% of the estimated $1.5 million project. This 2029 project may be moved up to 2028. Michael noted over $9 million in grant funding has been secured in the past 4-5 years. Updates were provided on Weston Avenue repairs by Integrity Grading, Scoffield Avenue repairs by HOS Construction, and Fuller Street repairs by Melvin. A special assessment meeting for Bladel Avenue was tentatively scheduled for May 28, 2026 at 6:00 PM.

Staff reported on road limit removal, mailbox and sign repairs, dewatering operations near Weston Avenue tributary lasting about a week, spring curb line sweeping, hot mix patching, storm damage cleanup from East Nick area, and inlet repairs on Birch Street. Significant flooding issues were discussed with potential permanent pump solutions being explored. Equipment maintenance and changeover from winter to summer operations was detailed.

Josh reported on water service repairs, well maintenance, and ongoing meter card maintenance program. Sanitary side experienced high pump activity with several pump pulls for maintenance and power failures at lift stations. A major issue discussed was clear water discharge into sanitary sewers - in April, 37 million gallons were put into the water system but 53-54 million gallons were treated at the wastewater plant, indicating approximately 15 million gallons of clear water infiltration or illegal sump pump connections.

Michael presented the annual report showing operating revenues of approximately $3 million, net operating income of $482,911, and utility value of nearly $34 million (6.7% increase from infrastructure placed in service). Water loss was reported at 6%. The utility serves approximately 6,100 customers total - 5,250 in Weston, 830 in Rothschild, and 5 in other areas. Report was acknowledged unanimously.

Michael explained this League of Wisconsin Municipalities sample resolution supports long-term funding solutions for the state transportation fund to help local communities with recurring street maintenance and snow plowing costs currently subsidized by general tax dollars. Motion to recommend village board adoption passed unanimously.

Michael presented that King Gravel was the low bidder at $4.23/ton for crushing recycled asphalt and concrete at the Ryan Street site, with an estimated project total of $126,900 for 30,000 tons. Analysis showed this practice saves approximately $11/cubic yard compared to city of Wausau costs, totaling about $130,000 savings on this year's three projects. Motion to award contract to King Gravel passed unanimously.

Staff sought guidance on enforcement of violations for residents discharging clear water (sump pumps, footing drains) into sanitary sewers. Committee discussed distinguishing between unknowing versus willful violations. Staff will work with In-Pipe Technologies to identify problem areas. Motion passed unanimously to direct staff to create enforcement language with fines not exceeding $100/day for violations.

Michael presented a request to allow a duplex at 2230 Radkkey Avenue to temporarily connect via long lateral until Radkkey Avenue reconstruction. Committee added language requiring reconstruction and assessment payment no later than end of 2029 construction season. Motion passed unanimously.

Agreement with Village of Rothschild for construction from Wolfman Street to Helin Street was presented. Weston's share for water and sewer work is estimated at $94,860. Steam Construction is the contractor. Motion to recommend approval passed unanimously.

discussions.item: Construction Services for Alderson/Jelnik and Bladel Projects
discussions.body: Michael presented proposals for construction inspection services. MSA was recommended for Alderson/Jelnik roundabout project at $37,315, and Clark Dietz for Bladel Avenue at $73,730 due to having full-time experienced staff versus interns. Motion to approve both recommendations passed unanimously.

actionItems: Staff to finalize Birch Street grant project timeline and evaluate 2028 CIP adjustments
Schedule Bladel Avenue special assessment meeting for May 28, 2026 at 6:00 PM
Staff to draft enforcement language for sanitary sewer discharge violations with $100/day fine structure
Award recycled asphalt crushing contract to King Gravel for $126,900
Enter agreement with 2230 Radkkey Avenue property owner for temporary utility connection with 2029 assessment deadline
Approve agreement with Rothschild for Military Road construction - Weston share $94,860
Approve MSA for Alderson/Jelnik construction services and Clark Dietz for Bladel Avenue construction services
Norcon to begin Scoffield Avenue concrete repairs starting Wednesday/Thursday
Water main flushing to begin following week after notice distribution

title: Tourism Commission
overview: The Village of Weston Tourism Commission held its first meeting following reorganization, electing officers and approving a $5,000 grant for the Taste and Glow Balloonfest. The commission learned that a competing balloon event in Marshfield was canceled, potentially increasing attendance at the Weston event.

agenda.item: Call to order and Pledge of Allegiance
New business - Grant request for Taste and Glow Balloonfest
Remarks from staff and commission members

discussions.body: Trusty Erling was nominated and elected as Chair unanimously. Cushing was nominated for Vice Chair, seconded by Jackson, and elected unanimously. Renee was nominated and elected as Secretary unanimously. There was brief procedural confusion about whether the Vice Chair nomination had been seconded.
Minutes from the April 21st meeting were approved unanimously. The motion was seconded by Renee.

discussions.item: Grant Request - Taste and Glow Balloonfest
discussions.body: Brandon Jill of 3308 Green Pastures Lane presented on behalf of Taste and Glow Balloonfest. He reported approximately 11,000-12,000 attendees last year and announced that a competing Marshfield balloon event was canceled due to permit issues, making them the sole hot air balloon event in the area. Commission members questioned room tracking, noting the application showed 20 fewer rooms than last year. The commission approved a $5,000 grant matching last year's amount, with a request for better hotel room tracking data in future reports. Motion passed unanimously.

Renee reported on attending the Alice in Dairyland dinner at Red Rock Orchard in Edgar. She described meeting the candidates and noted that Anastasia, who sat at their table, won the competition. The candidates toured Marathon County attractions including Mullen's Cheese. This was the first time the event had been hosted in Marathon County since the 1970s.

actionItems: Approved $5,000 grant to Taste and Glow Balloonfest for their annual event
Taste and Glow Balloonfest requested to provide hotel room usage data in their post-event report
Next meeting scheduled for July 20th at 4:00 PM

title: Plan Commission
overview: The Village of Weston Plan Commission approved a site plan for the new Mountain Bay Metropolitan Police Department and Municipal Court facility at 3600 Community Drive, and adopted a new salt storage shed inspection program for wellhead protection districts. Both items passed unanimously with all commissioners present voting in favor.

agenda.item: Call to order and roll call
Site plan review - Mountain Bay Metro Police Department and Municipal Court facility at 3600 Community Center Drive
Salt storage shed inspection program for wellhead protection district
April 2026 staff approved certified survey maps and site plans
Planning and development department monthly project update

discussions.item: Mountain Bay Metro Police Department and Municipal Court facility site plan
discussions.body: Staff presented the site plan for the new police department and municipal court at 3600 Community Center Drive, noting it meets all B2 district zoning requirements. Police Chief Jeremy explained the building was rotated to face east due to a gas pipeline easement running under the parking lot, which significantly reduced fill costs. Commissioner Garant asked about future expansion capability, and the chief confirmed the building could expand north or south away from the gas line, with capacity for 50-60 officers versus current staffing of 40. Discussion covered an alternate storage shed placement, with suggestions to locate it further north to accommodate future expansion. The motion to recommend approval to the board of trustees with staff conditions 2-5 passed unanimously.

discussions.item: Salt storage shed inspection program for wellhead protection district
discussions.body: Staff proposed a new inspection program with two annual inspections per facility - a warm weather inspection focusing on structural integrity and drainage, and a winter operational inspection focusing on material handling and containment. The program uses a standardized worksheet similar to DOT standards. Staff noted one existing facility near Sternberg has never gone through the wellhead protection conditional use process and predates the ordinance. Commissioners agreed staff should reach out to that facility about compliance. Commissioner Lumper praised the well-organized program. The motion to approve the program with reporting back to both Plan Commission and Public Works Committee passed unanimously.

discussions.item: April 2026 staff approved items and building permits
discussions.body: Both the April 2026 staff approved certified survey maps/site plans and the April 2026 building permits were approved unanimously with no discussion.

discussions.item: Planning department monthly update and future items
discussions.body: Staff announced the first meeting of the joint Plan Commission-Village Board steering committee for the comprehensive plan is scheduled for Monday, June 1st at 5:00 p.m. Discussion arose about residential TIF districts for workforce housing, with staff noting Ehlers has prepared a presentation on qualifications that could be shared with commissioners or incorporated into the comprehensive plan steering committee discussions.

publicComment: Jim Pensel of 5002 Arrow Street, Weston, spoke via online connection. He urged the commission to exercise common sense regarding landscaping requirements for the police department project, referencing previous relaxed standards for the Greenheck Turner building, so taxpayers don't have to spend excessive dollars on landscaping points. He also requested annual reporting on salt shed inspections in wellhead protection zones, noting some zoning complaints are years old and get 'lost in the sauce.'
actionItems: Site plan for Mountain Bay Metro Police Department and Municipal Court at 3600 Community Center Drive recommended for approval to Board of Trustees with staff conditions
Salt storage shed inspection program adopted for wellhead protection district with twice-annual inspections
Staff to report inspection findings to both Plan Commission and Public Works Committee next year
Staff to reach out to existing salt storage facility near Sternberg that has not gone through wellhead protection conditional use process
Joint Plan Commission-Village Board steering committee meeting for comprehensive plan scheduled for June 1st at 5:00 p.m.
Electric vehicle charging stations noted as future agenda item for fall
Staff to share Ehlers presentation on residential TIF district qualifications with commissioners

title: Health & Human Services Committee Meeting
overview: The Marathon County Health & Human Services Committee held an educational orientation meeting for new and returning board members, featuring presentations from department heads on the Health Department, Social Services, Veteran Services, and Aging Disability Resource Center. No action items were voted on besides approval of minutes; the meeting focused on introducing committee members to department missions, budgets, and current initiatives.

agenda.item: Call to order and remote attendance confirmation
Approval of April 1st committee meeting minutes
Operational functions required by statute - no items
Review of standing committee ordinance section 2.04
Health Department presentation by Caitlyn Suginaka
Social Services Department overview by Administrator Leonard
Veteran Services Department presentation by Erin Galindo
Aging Disability Resource Center presentation by Mike Ray

discussions.body: Supervisor Cavlli made a motion to approve the minutes, seconded by Vice Chair Rosenberg. The motion passed unanimously with all members voting in favor.

discussions.item: Review of standing committee ordinance and jurisdiction
discussions.body: Administrator Lance Leonard walked committee members through section 2.04 sub-5 of county ordinances, explaining the committee's mission, statutory responsibilities, and reporting relationships with departments including Health, Social Services, Veterans Services, and affiliated multi-county entities like North Central Health Care and the Aging Disability Resource Center. He explained that subordinate bodies like the Social Services Board and Veteran Service Commission provide input but don't directly supervise department heads.

Health Officer Caitlyn Suginaka presented the department's four divisions: environmental health, nursing, community health improvement, and administration. She highlighted their 1,200+ licenses for restaurants and public facilities, the Nurse Family Partnership program graduating its first two-year-olds, a new homeless system response position funded by the county board, and public health vending machines stocked with free resources including tick kits. The department has approximately 37.6 FTE with about half their $5 million budget coming from tax levy and the rest from grants and fees.

Administrator Leonard presented on behalf of absent director Christa, noting the department has nearly 140 FTE with a $22 million budget but only $7 million from tax levy. He highlighted the successful Family Keys housing stability pilot program that addresses root causes of housing instability for child welfare and youth justice clients. He noted potential restructuring requests coming for children's long-term support and child support units due to increased need.

Veteran Service Officer Erin Galindo reported his small team of 3.0 FTE had 8,149 interactions in 2025, up from 5,900 the prior year. He noted $44 million annually flows to Marathon County veterans through VA benefits, with $2.6 million in retroactive payments in 2025 alone. He discussed suicide prevention efforts after 7 of the county's 30 suicides in 2025 were veterans, housing stability partnerships with Center for Veterans Issues, and outreach to surviving spouses for dependency and indemnity compensation benefits.

Executive Director Mike Ray explained the ADRC serves four counties (Marathon, Lincoln, Langlade, Wood) with about 60 FTE and 385-400 volunteers. He detailed challenges with the nutrition/Meals on Wheels program, noting federal funding has been flat while food costs increased 40-45% over four years, forcing reductions in meals served and staffing. Marathon County's levy contribution is $413,135, with the ADRC not requesting a levy increase from 2011 until 2024.

In response to Supervisor Kremes' question about pandemic preparedness related to a norovirus outbreak on a cruise ship, Health Officer Suginaka confirmed the department has plans in place including measles response training, receives alerts about potential exposures from state partners, and has staff trained in emergency response from COVID experience. She noted funding would likely come from staff reallocation in short-term emergencies.

actionItems: April 1st meeting minutes approved unanimously
North Central Healthcare to be scheduled for separate full presentation before the committee
ADRC Executive Director Mike Ray to present annual report with 2025 data to full board
Strategic plan discussion to be added to upcoming agenda by Chair Boots
Next meeting corrected to Tuesday, June 3rd at 3:00 PM (not May 29th as initially stated)

title: Board of Trustees
overview: The Village of Weston Board of Trustees approved increased park and recreation impact fees, passed several ordinances including mobile food vendor licensing and e-bike regulations, and discussed ongoing concerns about the farmers market relocation to Kennedy Park. The board also adjourned the Board of Review to June 15, 2026 and made appointments to the CLIPS committee.

agenda.item: Board of Review adjournment to June 15, 2026
Board of Trustees meeting called to order and roll call
Public hearing on park and recreation impact fees (Ordinance 26-010 and Resolution 2026-015)
Approval of minutes from previous meetings
Acknowledge reports from boards, committees, and commissions with chair updates
Consent agenda including vouchers and license renewals
Ordinances including mobile food vendor, nuisances, e-bikes, and transportation funding resolution
Committee appointments and farmers market discussion

discussions.body: Staff presented proposed fee increases from $300 to $761 for single family units, $250 to $563 for duplex units, and $250 to $495 for multifamily units, based on the 2022 public facilities needs assessment. Jim Pitel spoke during public comment questioning the lack of detailed operational cost data justifying the fees. Trustee Barb supported the fee increase citing population growth and fair cost sharing for new residents. Ordinance 26-010 was approved with motion by Lope Sorrell, second by Zagami, passing unanimously. Resolution 2026-015 was approved with motion by Hermling, second by Clark, passing unanimously.

Chief Finkey explained this ordinance creates a regional inspection agreement with Marshfield, Wisconsin Rapids, Stevens Point, Clover Belt, and other municipalities. When any participating fire department inspects a food truck and places a sticker, other communities will accept that inspection rather than requiring separate inspections for each municipality. Ordinance 26-008 was approved with motion by Lope, second by Zagami, passing unanimously.

The board debated amendments to section 1.08 of the elected and appointed village officials handbook. Discussion centered on removing language requiring specific justifications (health, safety, welfare) for remote attendance. Trustees Beck and others argued the policy should simply require notification, camera use, and exclusion from closed sessions without chairs having to evaluate excuses. An amendment to strike sentences requiring justification passed with Trustees Hermling and Motti voting no. The amended policy then passed with the same two dissenting votes.

The president appointed Stephanie Daniels (currently on Finance and HR) and Golden Mua to the CLIPS committee. Golden Mua expressed interest in Finance committee but will start with CLIPS. Motion by Hermling, second by Lopes, approved unanimously. The board discussed having committee application resumes included in future packets for transparency.

discussions.item: Farmers Market Location at Kennedy Park
discussions.body: Discussion-only item addressing the relocation from the former Camp Phillips/Schofield Avenue location after property owner Ray Lockery developed that site. Trustee Beck raised safety concerns about cars parking on grass along Alderson and in the park. Renee explained historical context: original location had 25 stalls, Camp Phillips location had 42 stalls without vehicle parking, and Kennedy Park now has 53 spots with vendor vehicle parking. She noted they receive approximately $5,000 in grants for SNAP/EBT token programs and the market operates without financial loss. No action was taken; discussion will continue.

Schofield Coin and Hobby LLC at 5703 Jamar Street requested secondhand article and jewelry dealer licenses. Chief Hunt explained these involve precious metals transactions with significant dollar amounts requiring tracking for stolen merchandise prevention. The license was approved with an amendment requiring satisfactory background check completion and receipt of occupancy permit, as the building is still under construction. Passed unanimously.

Jim Pitel criticized during public comment the estimated $108,000 cost for two outside engineering consultants, arguing the village should fill the vacant engineering technician position instead. Public Works Director Michael Dolan later explained the position has been advertised multiple times but the specific skillset is scarce in central Wisconsin, with even consulting firms struggling to find local candidates.

publicComment: Jim Pitel of 52nd Street spoke twice. During general public comment, he criticized the $108,000 expenditure for outside engineering consultants and questioned why the engineering technician position remains unfilled, demanding administrator accountability. He also objected to Lisa Beck being introduced as a Safer board member before official appointment, called for all committee discussions to occur publicly, and complained about lack of Kennedy Park funding information in the packet. During the impact fee hearing, he questioned the lack of detailed operational cost data for parks maintenance to justify the fee increases.
actionItems: Park and recreation impact fees increased effective with ordinance 26-010 and resolution 2026-015
Board of Review adjourned to June 15, 2026
Mobile food vendor regional inspection agreement ordinance 26-008 adopted
E-bike/e-moto regulations ordinance 26-012 adopted
Nuisance ordinance 26-011 amendments adopted
Remote meeting attendance policy amended to remove justification requirements
Stephanie Daniels and Golden Mua appointed to CLIPS committee
Schofield Coin and Hobby LLC secondhand dealer license approved pending background check and occupancy permit
Compliance assurance plan (CAP) approved
Staff to prepare intergovernmental service agreement for building inspections assistance to Town of Ringle
Comprehensive Plan steering committee meeting scheduled for Monday, June 1st at 6:00 PM
Speed limit study on Weston Avenue to be brought back to the board
Committee application resumes to be included in future board packets

title: Regular Meeting
overview: The Marathon County Board of Supervisors held an adjourned organizational meeting where they approved committee appointments, elected Supervisors Hapa and Arstead to the executive committee, and unanimously passed a resolution to reduce landfill tipping fees for severe weather debris from the recent tornado and flooding disaster. The board also approved consent agenda items and budget resolutions for social services and out-of-home placement reserves.

agenda.item: Pledge of Allegiance and moment of reflection
Election of at-large executive committee members
Resolution 26-26: Landfill tipping fee modification for storm debris

discussions.body: Chair Gibbs presented committee appointments developed with Vice Chair Boots, emphasizing goals of increasing diversity of perspectives, allowing supervisor growth, and honoring preferences. Over 34% of the board is in their first or second term. The motion by Supervisor Cavelli, seconded by Supervisor Crims, included both county board chair and administrator appointments. The motion carried but was not unanimous.

discussions.item: Election of at-large executive committee members
discussions.body: Three supervisors were nominated: Supervisor Hapa (nominated by Supervisor Mask), Supervisor Leur (nominated by Supervisor Rosenberg, who cited her advocacy for children and elders), and Supervisor Arstead (nominated by Supervisor Boots). Paper ballots were distributed for supervisors to select two candidates. Supervisors Hapa and Arstead were elected to the executive committee.

Administrator Leonard and Director Hagen Busher presented a proposal from the Solid Waste Management Board to reduce tipping fees for debris from the recent tornado and flooding disaster. The state is waiving $13.10/ton and Ringle is waiving $2.33/ton. The county proposed reducing fees to approximately $36.94/ton (commercial rate minus state and local waivers) to cover basic costs. Two residents, Jessica Toasty and Matt Haybeck, were granted special dispensation to speak, requesting Marathon County waive fees entirely and extend the timeframe beyond 60 days. Administrator Leonard mentioned exploring a partnership with the Community Foundation to help reimburse uninsured costs. The board voted to suspend rules (passed unanimously with two-thirds majority) to consider the resolution, then approved Resolution 26-26 unanimously.
Motion by Supervisor Morash, seconded by Supervisor Robinson. The resolution was approved but not unanimously with no discussion.
Motion by Supervisor P, seconded by Supervisor Robinson. The resolution was approved but not unanimously with no discussion.

publicComment: Two residents were granted special dispensation to speak on the landfill tipping fee resolution. Jessica Toasty requested Marathon County waive fees entirely for six months, noting the state, DNR, and Ringle are all waiving their portions, and emphasized that the community has been self-reliant with minimal county financial assistance. Matt Haybeck spoke on behalf of residents with demolished homes, noting many insurance adjusters haven't arrived yet and asking for fee relief and extended timeframes.
actionItems: Committee appointments approved as presented by Chair Gibbs and Vice Chair Boots
Supervisors Hapa and Arstead elected to executive committee at-large positions
Resolution 26-26 approved: reduced landfill tipping fees for storm debris through June 17th (aligned with state emergency declaration)
Administrator to work with Community Foundation on mechanism to reimburse uninsured debris disposal costs
Solid Waste Management Board to develop formal policy within six months for handling similar disaster situations
Resolution 24-26 approved: out-of-home placement reserve increase
Resolution 25-26 approved: social services budget amendment

title: Environmental Resources Committee Meeting
overview: The Marathon County Environmental Resources Committee approved two rezoning petitions and voted to forward a modified fee waiver resolution to the full county board to assist tornado victims, limiting the waiver to septic system reconnect fees only. The committee also received educational presentations from Solid Waste, Parks Recreation and Forestry, and Conservation Planning and Zoning departments.

agenda.item: Call to order and Pledge of Allegiance
Public hearing: Tim Veland rezoning petition (Urban Residential/General Agriculture to Rural Residential, Town of Green Valley)
Public hearing: Jeffrey and Cynthia Schmidtz rezoning petition (Conservancy Recreation to General Agriculture, Town of McMillan)
Consideration of fee waivers for properties impacted by April 17, 2026 severe weather
Solid Waste Department presentation by Dave Hagenbusher
Parks, Recreation and Forestry Department presentation by Tom Lovelin

discussions.body: Supervisor Sefeld made a motion to approve the minutes, seconded by Supervisor Drebeck. The motion carried unanimously with no discussion.

discussions.item: Tim Veland rezoning petition (Town of Green Valley)
discussions.body: Land Use Specialist Garrett Pogle presented the staff report for rezoning from Urban Residential and General Agriculture to Rural Residential for a proposed land division. The Town of Green Valley recommended approval on March 10, 2026. No public testimony was offered. Supervisor Drebeck moved to approve based on findings of fact, conclusions of law, and town approval. Supervisor Kurt seconded. Motion carried unanimously.

discussions.item: Jeffrey and Cynthia Schmidtz rezoning petition (Town of McMillan)
discussions.body: Garrett Pogle presented the petition to rezone from Conservancy Recreation to General Agriculture. Supervisor Leur asked about the conservancy nature, and Pogle explained the property contains a home and horse riding stable, with the rezoning needed to make the home a conforming use. The Town of McMillan recommended approval on April 20, 2026. No public testimony was offered. Supervisor Sefeld moved to approve, Supervisor Drew seconded. Motion carried unanimously.

discussions.item: Fee waivers for April 17, 2026 severe weather victims
discussions.body: Director Lori Mskimmons presented a proposal to waive county zoning and septic fees for properties damaged by the April 17 tornado affecting Ringle, Kronenwetter, Easton, and Weston. Significant debate ensued. Supervisor Drew questioned fairness to future individual disaster victims not covered by emergency declarations. Supervisor Leur asked about levy impacts. Supervisor Drebeck expressed concern about setting precedent and proposed an amendment limiting waivers to reconnect fees only ($160 each). The amendment passed, and the amended resolution was approved to forward to the county board, though not unanimously.

Director Dave Hagenbusher provided an overview of landfill operations, explaining the facility accepts about 1,000 tons daily with 11 FTEs. He highlighted the renewable natural gas partnership with Vidi Energy generating revenue, significant capital expenses of $4-6 million for expansions and closures, and new state requirements for leachate treatment by 2032. Marilyn Bend praised the department for helping Ringle tornado victims and keeping private landfill costs competitive.

discussions.item: Parks, Recreation and Forestry Department presentation
discussions.body: Tom Lovelin, County Forest Administrator, presented on behalf of Director Jamie who had an emergency. He explained the department operates both county and City of Wausau park systems with 44 full-time and 120 seasonal employees. A Nine Mile County Forest study showed out-of-town visitors generate $3.25 million annually for Marathon County's economy. Supervisor Sefeld noted this was Lovelin's last committee meeting before his June 1st retirement after nearly 30 years of service.

actionItems: Rezoning petition for James Offer and David Tract (Urban Residential/General Agriculture to Rural Residential in Town of Green Valley) approved and forwarded to County Board
Rezoning petition for Jeffrey and Cynthia Schmidtz (Conservancy Recreation to General Agriculture in Town of McMillan) approved and forwarded to County Board
Modified fee waiver resolution limiting waivers to septic reconnect fees only for April 17, 2026 tornado victims forwarded to County Board for consideration
Track costs and requests related to fee waivers for future policy development
CPZ to present conservation topics at future summer educational meeting

title: Parks Committee
overview: The Village of Weston Parks Committee deferred action on a controversial dog day at the aquatic center proposal, approved installation of new kayak launch signs along the Eau Claire River, and received updates on Kennedy Park development and Yellow Banks disc golf course improvements. Public commenters criticized the 4 p.m. meeting time and questioned Kennedy Park fundraising transparency.

agenda.item: Call to order and Pledge of Allegiance
Kennedy Park update and timeline presentation
Yellow Banks disc golf course update
Dog day at the aquatic center discussion
Graffiti on Eau Claire River Trail discussion
Topics for future meetings and staff remarks

discussions.body: Minutes from the April 27, 2026 meeting were approved with a motion and second. The motion carried with no opposition.

Staff presented a timeline document showing the project history from the Comprehensive Outdoor Recreation Plan approval in November 2023 through the May 9th ribbon cutting for youth baseball fields. Director Oster explained the village planned to contribute approximately $7 million of the $14.3 million total project for infrastructure like bathrooms, shelter, and wellhouse. Committee member Scott asked clarifying questions about fundraising timelines, learning there is no fixed deadline but a 10-year evaluation point. Staff noted the Friends of Kennedy Park are currently fundraising for field lights, having approximately $85,000 of the $117,000 goal, and plan a public check presentation at their June tournament.

discussions.item: Yellow Banks disc golf course update
discussions.body: Dan provided photos and maps showing fallen trees removed from the Eau Claire River and wood mats installed over gas pipelines to protect them during logging operations. The mats are approximately 8x8 inch wood construction owned by the village, with extras available for future trail projects. TC Energy (formerly ANR pipeline) conducted site visits during the work.

discussions.item: Dog day at the aquatic center
discussions.body: The committee deferred this item to next month because committee chair Katrina was absent due to an unforeseen issue. A resident named Andy who attended specifically to oppose allowing dogs in the public pool stated 'There should not be animals allowed into a public pool. Period.' and said he may return next month. The committee noted Andy's objection for the record.

discussions.item: Graffiti on Eau Claire River Trail
discussions.body: Staff explained challenges removing graffiti from concrete benches and garbage cans on the trail, noting they cannot get pressure washing equipment down there due to narrow bridges and steep ditches. They discussed potential solutions including anti-vandalism coatings and organizing volunteer work days. Committee members suggested community involvement similar to Friends groups, noting high trail usage. No formal action was taken; staff will research cleaning options.

Dan presented version 7 of new kayak launch signs for the Eau Claire River, covering eight launch sites including three in Village of Weston (Highway J, Ross, and Yellow Banks). Signs are funded through Marathon County Parks Foundation and state grants at no cost to the village. The committee approved a motion for the village to install the signs. Motion carried unanimously.

Director Oster reported aquatic center pool repairs were completed, filling began and should be done by end of day Wednesday, with opening day June 6th. Dugout painting at Kennedy Park was completed. Staff announced the Mock Miller Park master plan process will begin with a kickoff meeting with Rettler next Tuesday, and the last bike and pedestrian plan open house is June 11th from 5-7 p.m.

publicComment: Two public comments were offered. Andy (6504 [address inaudible]) spoke in person opposing dogs at the aquatic center, stating 'There should not be animals allowed into a public pool. Period. I like dogs, but it's not a place for them. We have rivers. We have lakes. That's where they belong.' Jim Pensan (5002 [Street] Weston), speaking online under the name 'Vern M', criticized the 4 p.m. meeting time as demonstrating 'zero desire for interaction and input from the general public.' He also questioned Kennedy Park finances, calling the $8,700 expenditure figure 'too coincidental' and asking about the $10,000 grant and glow event funds, stating the numbers are 'extremely convoluted and misleading.'
actionItems: Dog day at aquatic center discussion deferred to June meeting when chair Katrina can be present
Approved installation of Eau Claire River kayak launch signs at Yellow Banks, Ross Avenue, and Highway J locations
Staff to research anti-vandalism coatings and cleaning options for Eau Claire River Trail benches
Food allergy awareness playground signage to be added to future agenda for discussion
Mock Miller Park master plan kickoff meeting with Rettler scheduled for next Tuesday
Next meeting scheduled for June 22, 2026

title: Finance and Human Resources Committee
overview: The Village of Weston Finance and Human Resources Committee approved previous meeting minutes and acknowledged monthly financial reports. The meeting featured an extensive educational presentation by Municipal Clerk Pam on the clerk's office operations, elections, licensing, and budget. Committee member Evan announced his resignation as he is moving out of the village.

agenda.item: Call to order and Pledge of Allegiance
Approval of minutes from previous Finance and HR meeting
TIF 1 and TIF 2 detail reports for April
Educational presentation - Municipal Clerk's Office budget and operations
Old business, new business, staff remarks

discussions.item: Approval of minutes from previous Finance and HR meeting
discussions.body: Motion to approve made by Ernling, seconded by Daniels. Passed unanimously with no discussion.

discussions.item: TIF 1 and TIF 2 detail reports for April
discussions.body: Jessica from finance discussed the timeline for TIF closure, anticipating October-November for final bond payoff. She explained the CDA will need to pass a resolution to call the bonds, which are due October 1st. A committee member asked about final numbers, and Jessica confirmed they will be able to pay off the debt.
Motion to acknowledge the April budget status report, TIF detail reports, and legal details report made by Daniels, seconded by Soaper. Passed unanimously.

Municipal Clerk Pam delivered an extensive presentation on clerk's office operations. She noted the office went from four full-time staff to currently two full-time and one part-time employee, with plans to add another part-time worker. She covered election administration including Badger Book electronic poll books ($11,000 for four units), licensing operations (51 liquor licenses, bartender licenses), tax collection (84% or $22 million collected at the clerk's office counter), pet licensing brought back in-house saving approximately $5,495 annually, and the quarterly newsletter costing approximately $30,000 per year. Discussion included poll worker wages being average at $12/hour but chief inspectors being on the lower end at current rates. Pet licensing fees increased from $10 to $20 due to Marathon County raising fees to municipalities to cover Humane Society costs.

Staff member Renee reported the farmers market at Kennedy Park has been going well, with full vendor capacity including food trucks, coffee vendors, craft people, and some produce vendors with greenhouses. A committee member who spoke with vendors reported they were very happy with the setup and location. Discussion touched on parking concerns on Alderson and Newport streets, with staff noting east side of Alderson is signed no parking while west side now allows parking.

Committee member Evan announced he is stepping down from the committee as he and his wife are moving away from Weston, though staying in the metro area. He thanked the committee for his time serving. The chair noted there are now open spots on this committee and others, asking members to reach out to citizens who may be interested in participating.

actionItems: CDA to pass resolution to call TIF bonds, anticipated this summer before October 1st deadline
Clerk's office seeking to hire one more part-time employee
Chief inspector pay increase to $15/hour to be proposed at next election term
Committee seeking citizen volunteers to fill open committee positions
Next meeting scheduled for Monday, June 15th at 4:30

title: Community Life and Public Safety
overview: The Village of Weston Community Life and Public Safety Committee recommended approval of nuisance ordinance amendments and e-bike regulations (with emoto restrictions added), discussed overnight parking regulations without action, and authorized demolition of an abandoned structure at 4104 Shore Avenue. The committee also approved a mobile food vendor fire inspection program and addressed concerns about crosswalk installation near Mount Olive Church.

agenda.item: Call to order and Pledge of Allegiance
Approval of minutes from previous meeting
Acknowledgment of department reports (Code Enforcement, Fire/EMS, Police)
Discussion on proposed amendments to Chapter 50 Nuisances
Discussion on proposed e-bicycle ordinance in Chapter 82
Discussion on overnight parking regulations October 31 to May 1
Discussion on crosswalk request on Alderson Street
Discussion on mobile food vendor fire inspection requirements
Discussion on address corrections for E911 compliance
Discussion on raze order for 4104 Shore Avenue

discussions.body: Staff reported that 3020 Mountain View Avenue will be removed from the code enforcement list after the property was auctioned and the new owner submitted repair permits. Chief Hunt reported the police department handled 2,394 calls in March (77 per day) with 281 citations and 410 traffic stops. The department has hired its 40th officer, with the 38th starting last week, 39th starting in May, and 40th around January. Two SROs work on zoning violations during summer for all three municipalities. Motion by Chwanka to acknowledge reports 5, 6, and 7 passed unanimously.

The committee reviewed final amendments including the tree limb height requirements (14 feet over streets, 10 feet over walkways) that were switched per previous discussion, plus language about compliance with jurisdiction authority. Motion by Olsen, seconded by Campkey, to recommend approval to the board of trustees passed unanimously.

Extensive discussion focused on emotos (electric mini-motorcycles). Chief Hunt strongly advocated for prohibiting emotos on roadways, sidewalks, and trails, comparing them to unlicensed dirt bikes and citing safety concerns. Committee discussed whether electric scooters should be included and how to handle discretion for children's play scooters. Chief Hunt noted there is currently no ordinance to regulate emotos. Motion by Olsen to recommend approval with the addition prohibiting emotos on roadways, sidewalks, trails, and any public property, seconded by Chwanka, passed unanimously.

Trustee Tolson presented research showing Weston's 12-hour no-parking window (4 AM to 4 PM) is far outside other municipalities' 3.5-5 hour windows. DPW Director Michael Wodalski noted plowing typically doesn't reach residential streets until 7-8 AM anyway. Chief Hunt advocated for keeping overnight parking restrictions to give officers time to clear vehicles before plowing. Committee discussed uniformity concerns and whether to use calendar date or house address for even/odd rules. No action taken; Michael Wodalski will develop a proposal identifying specific problem areas for the July meeting.

Mount Olive Church requested a crosswalk between the church and DC Everest parking areas. Committee discussed costs ($2,000-3,000 for basic crosswalk, $25,000-30,000 for lighted system). Chief Hunt reported no known incidents in the area. Committee consensus was that the church should pay for installation since it primarily benefits their congregation. Motion by Olsen that if Mount Olive is willing to cover the cost, the committee recommends board approval, seconded by Chwanka, passed unanimously. Michael Wodalski will contact Mount Olive.

Fire Chief explained a regional inspection program where Wisconsin Rapids, Marshfield, Stevens Point, Plover, Rib Mountain, and SAFER departments share inspections - one sticker is valid across all participating municipalities. Wausau opted out to do their own inspections. Chief noted a recent inspection revealed significant safety violations including improper LP piping and unsecured propane containers. Motion by Chwanka to approve Ordinance 26-8, seconded by Campkey, passed unanimously.

Staff presented three addressing corrections: 5915 Delicowski Street needs to become 5915 and 5917 for a duplex (owner has agreed); 3913 and 3917 Ross Avenue require renumbering due to sequence errors and a discovered secondary unit; and 2211 Radtkey Avenue needs correction for a new duplex on a private drive. Chief Hunt mentioned an additional issue at Weston Villa Apartments where an 8-plex has separate addresses for each unit. Motion passed unanimously to recommend approval to the board.

Staff requested authorization to begin raze proceedings on an abandoned structure that has been vacant since 2010-2011. A fire training burn was previously rejected as unsafe due to the structure's condition. The owners had housed chickens inside the building. Owners are current on taxes and prefer the raze order approach. Costs will be placed on the tax roll for recovery. Motion by Olsen, seconded by Campkey, passed unanimously.

publicComment: Jim Senalt spoke regarding several agenda items. He criticized the new farmers market location at the park, predicting grass damage from vehicle parking and questioning whether village code prohibits parking on grass. He noted ADA-accessible parking spaces placed on grass rather than asphalt may not meet requirements for firm, level surfaces. He questioned the origin of the new fire inspection requirement for mobile food vendors. He raised concerns about food truck permitting on public property versus the previous private property arrangement, and ambiguity about which days constitute 'farmers market days.' He also criticized the code enforcement report, stating some violations have been pending for years with no visible progress and requested regular status updates on older cases.
actionItems: Recommend approval of Chapter 50 Nuisance amendments to board of trustees
Recommend approval of e-bike ordinance with emoto prohibition on all public property to board of trustees
Michael Wodalski to develop proposal identifying specific problem areas for overnight parking restrictions for July meeting
Michael Wodalski to contact Mount Olive Church regarding cost-sharing for crosswalk installation
Recommend approval of Ordinance 26-8 for mobile food vendor fire inspections to board of trustees
Recommend approval of address corrections for E911 compliance to board of trustees
Authorize staff to begin raze order proceedings for 4104 Shore Avenue
Staff to investigate property condition complaint at Sternberg and South Timber intersection

title: Committee of the Whole Meeting
overview: Based on the published agenda, the Wausau School District Board of Education's Committee of the Whole meeting on May 18, 2026 was scheduled to address multiple action items including the preliminary 2026-27 budget, employee handbook updates, meal price changes, and policy revisions. The board was also expected to consider approval of the Riverview HVAC and roofing project and updates to the activities and athletics code.

agenda.item: Excellence in Action: Thomas Jefferson Elementary
Policy 9270 - Home-Based, Private, or Tribal Schooling (Action Requested)
Policy 2431.01 - High School Activities Code (Action Requested)

discussions.item: Excellence in Action: Thomas Jefferson Elementary
discussions.body: The board was scheduled to recognize Thomas Jefferson Elementary as part of the Excellence in Action program. No additional details or presenter information were provided in the agenda.

Tabatha Gundrum was scheduled to present the Employee Handbook updates for the 2026-27 school year in an estimated 5 minutes. The board was expected to review and consider approval of the updated handbook.

Elizabeth Channel, Assistant Superintendent of Operations, was scheduled to present the preliminary budget for 2026-27 in an estimated 10 minutes. The board was expected to consider the initial budget recommendation for the upcoming school year.
Karen Fochs was scheduled to present proposed changes to the 2026-2027 paid meal prices in an estimated 5 minutes. Supporting documentation included a meal price action sheet and PLE tool.

Dr. Jon Tomski and BJ Brandt were scheduled to present suggested changes to the Athletic and Activities Code in an estimated 10 minutes. The board was expected to consider approval of the 2026-2027 Code of Conduct updates.

discussions.item: Policy 9270 - Home-Based, Private, or Tribal Schooling
discussions.body: Dr. Jon Tomski was scheduled to present a proposed change to Policy 9270 regarding Home-Based, Private, or Tribal Schooling in an estimated 5 minutes. Action was requested for this policy update.

Dr. Jon Tomski was scheduled to present proposed changes to Policy 2431.01 - High School Activities Code in an estimated 5 minutes. The board was expected to consider approval of these policy revisions.

Ryan Urmanski, Director of Buildings and Grounds, was scheduled to present the Riverview HVAC and roofing project in an estimated 10 minutes. The board was expected to consider approval of the RFP for this facility improvement project.

publicComment: A public and student comment period was included on the agenda.
actionItems: Board was expected to vote on Employee Handbook Updates for 2026-27
Board was expected to vote on the Preliminary 2026-27 Budget recommendation
Board was expected to vote on 2026-2027 Paid Meal Price Update
Board was expected to vote on Activities & Athletics Code changes
Board was expected to vote on Policy 9270 - Home-Based, Private, or Tribal Schooling revisions
Board was expected to vote on Policy 2431.01 - High School Activities Code changes
Board was expected to vote on Riverview RFP Approval for HVAC and roofing project

title: Special Meeting
overview: Based on the published agenda, this Special Meeting of the Wausau School District Board of Education was scheduled to address potential litigation in closed session under Wisconsin State Statute 19.85(g). The board was expected to hold preliminary discussions regarding a legal matter before potentially reconvening in open session to take any necessary action.

agenda.item: Request for Closed Session Pursuant to State Statutes
Reconvene in Open Session, to take further action if necessary and appropriate

discussions.body: The board was scheduled to enter closed session to hold preliminary discussions regarding potential litigation, as permitted under Wisconsin State Statute 19.85(g). This statute allows governmental bodies to convene in closed session when considering matters that could result in legal action against or on behalf of the district.
Following the closed session discussion, the board was expected to reconvene in open session to take any further action deemed necessary and appropriate based on the closed session deliberations.

publicComment: No public comment period was included on this agenda.
actionItems: Board was expected to potentially take action following the closed session discussion regarding potential litigation

title: Regular Meeting
overview: Based on the published agenda, the Wausau School District Board of Education was scheduled to address several significant action items at their May 11, 2026 regular meeting, including approval of the 2026-27 budget reconciliation plan, teacher contract renewals, and wage increases for non-teaching staff. The board was also expected to consider renewal of the WAMCS charter school contract for 2026-2031 and enter closed session regarding personnel matters including non-renewal notices and contract evaluations.

agenda.item: Pledge of Allegiance: Jim Bouche, President
Request for Closed Session Pursuant to State Statutes

discussions.body: The board was scheduled to recognize Maine Elementary as part of the district's Excellence in Action program, which typically highlights achievements and initiatives at individual schools.

Elizabeth Channel, Assistant Superintendent of Operations, was expected to present a summary report of all legal counsel expenses incurred during the third quarter of 2025-2026. The report was described as broken down by law firm and by type of legal advice sought, requiring no action as a written report with an estimated 2-minute presentation time.

Elizabeth Channel, Assistant Superintendent of Operations, was scheduled to present the budget reconciliation plan for budgeting purposes, previously introduced at the April Committee of the Whole meeting. The presentation was estimated at 3 minutes with action requested from the board.
Tabatha Gundrum was scheduled to present a list of teacher contracts for approval for the 2026-27 school year. The presentation was estimated at 5 minutes with action requested from the board.

Tabatha Gundrum was expected to present a recommendation for a 2.63% wage adjustment for all employee groups in the District except teachers, in accordance with the budget reconciliation plan. The agenda noted that teacher negotiations would commence soon with a separate action at a later date, with this presentation estimated at 5 minutes.

Elizabeth Channel, WAMCS Head of School, was scheduled to present the 2026-2031 contract renewal for the Wausau Area Montessori Charter School, previously introduced at the April Committee of the Whole meeting. The presentation was estimated at 2 minutes with action requested.

The board was scheduled to enter closed session pursuant to Wisconsin Statutes sections 19.85(1)(c), (e), and (g) to discuss final notice of non-renewal and contract evaluation matters, with plans to reconvene in open session to take further action if necessary.

publicComment: A public and student comment period was included on the agenda.
actionItems: Board was expected to vote on approval of the Consent Agenda including appointments, separations, leaves of absence, retirements, meeting minutes, payment of bills, and donations
Action was requested for the 2026-27 Budget Reconciliation Plan
Board was expected to vote on 2026-27 Teacher Contract Approvals
Action was requested for Various Group Wage/Salary Increase of 2.63% for non-teaching staff
Board was expected to vote on WAMCS Charter School Contract Renewal for 2026-2031
Board was expected to potentially take action following closed session regarding non-renewal notices and contract evaluations

The commission also received updates on lead service line replacements and wastewater projects.

The director reported on wastewater projects including the Headworks Screening Project, Cherry Street Lift Station, and Lift Station Assessment with Clark Dietz. This was an informational report with no action required.
The commission approved the 2027 through 2031 Capital Improvement Projects Budget for the Drinking Water and Wastewater Utility. The motion passed.

The commission discussed the final WPDES permit issued with a five-year term. This was a discussion item with no formal action taken.

title: City Council Meeting
overview: The Wausau Common Council approved mayoral appointments to multiple boards and commissions, passed a consent agenda including bike rack request procedures and special assessments for street construction, but saw contentious debate over proposed amendments to council standing rules with multiple failed motions. The council voted 11-0 on consent items while rule suspension votes were divided, with one failing 5-6.

agenda.item: Week of the Young Child Proclamation
Joint Resolution from the Bicycle & Pedestrian Advisory Committee and the Infrastructure & Facilities Committee Approving the Bike Rack Request Form
Resolution from the Infrastructure & Facilities Committee Levying Special Assessments for the 2025 Street Construction Projects
Ordinance from the Common Council to Amend Wausau Municipal Code Ch. 2.16, Standing Rules of the Common Council

Confirming Appointments of the Mayor of the City of Wausau to the Historic Preservation Commission, Sustainability, Energy & Environment Committee, Business Improvement District Board, Community Development Authority Board and Ethics Board

discussions.body: The council approved minutes from both the March 24, 2026 and April 14, 2026 regular meetings. The motion passed unanimously 11-0, moved by Sarah Watson and seconded by Tom Neal.

The consent agenda was approved 11-0, moved by Sarah Watson and seconded by Bruce Trueblood. This included the Joint Resolution Approving the Bike Rack Request Form from the Bicycle & Pedestrian Advisory Committee and Infrastructure & Facilities Committee, as well as the Resolution Levying Special Assessments for the 2025 Street Construction Projects.

The council voted to suspend Rule 11(A) regarding referral of ordinances, passing 8-3 with Matt Hoenecke, Lou Larson, and Bruce Trueblood voting no. However, the motion to suspend Rule 21 regarding amending of the rules failed 5-6, with Carol Lukens, Michael Martens, Andrew Wiskowski, Kristin Slonski, Sarah Watson, and Lou Larson voting against.

discussions.item: Ordinance to Amend Wausau Municipal Code Ch. 2.16, Standing Rules of the Common Council
discussions.body: This item saw extensive procedural debate with multiple votes. A motion to approve as amended failed, as did a motion to refer back to the Rules Review Committee. An amendment by Alder Watson subject to attorney review passed, as did a subsequent amendment by Alder Tierney. An amendment proposed by Alder Neal failed.

The council approved the Mayor's appointments to the Historic Preservation Commission, Sustainability, Energy & Environment Committee, Business Improvement District Board, Community Development Authority Board, and Ethics Board. The motion passed.

publicComment: Public comment was included on the agenda both before the business meeting (preregistered citizens) and after the business meeting.
